coredata.dc:title: Ultra-sensitive and selective Hg2+ detection based on fluorescent carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2013 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Ruihua
Li, Haitao
Kong, Weiqian
Liu, Juan
Liu, Yang
Tong, Cuiyan
Zhang, Xing
Kang, Zhenhui
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent carbon dots (FCDs) were efficiently synthesized by one-step sodium hydroxide-assisted reflux method from poly(ethylene glycol) (PEG). The obtained FCDs exhibit excellent water-solubility and high stability. Under the UV irradiation, the FCDs could emit bright blue photoluminescence, and also they were found to show excellent up-conversion fluorescence. It was further demonstrated that such FCDs can serve as effective fluorescent sensing platform for Hg2+ ions detection with ultra-sensitivity and selectivity. The sensing system achieved a limit of detection as low as 1fM, which is much lower than all the previous reported sensing systems for Hg2+ ions detection. This FCDs sensing system has been successfully applied for the analysis of Hg2+ ions in water samples from river, lake, and tap water, showing good practical feasibility.
coredata.dcterms:subject: A. Optical materials
B. Chemical synthesis
D. Optical properties

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ultra-sensitive and selective Hg2+ detection using FCDs
Efficient Hg2+ detection using FCDs in water samples from surroundings
Fluorescence quenching mechanism of FCDs-Hg2+ system

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Synthetic scheme of FCDs through the sodium hydroxide-assisted process and their fluorescent/ion detective properties. 1(b) TEM image of FCDs, inset is the size histogram of FCDs. (c–f) FL microscopy images of FCDs under different excitation wavelengths: (c–f) for 360nm, 390nm, 470nm, and 540nm, respectively.

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of the FCDs. (b) FTIR spectrum of the FCDs. (c) PL spectra of the FCDs under UV and visible excitations from 300nm to 600nm (emission in visible range). (d) The up-converted PL properties of the FCDs under NIR excitations (emission in visible range).

(a) PL spectra of FCDs solution with different Hg2+ ions concentrations (from 0 to 1μM, the lowest Hg2+ ions concentration is 1fM) under the excitation wavelength of 350nm. (b) The dependence of F/F
0 of FCDs solution on the concentrations of Hg2+ ions within the range of 0–5fM. The inset shows the dependence of F/F
0 on the concentrations of Hg2+ ions within the range of 0–10nM. (F and F 0 are the PL intensities at 450nm in the presence and absence of Hg2+ ions, respectively.).
(a,b) PL spectra, and the different PL intensity ratio (F/F 0) of the FCDs solution with various metal ions (at the same metal ions concentration of 1μM).
(a,b) PL spectra, and the different PL intensity ratio (F/F 0) of the FCDs with various Hg2+ ions concentrations in the range of 0–50fM in river water.
Schematic illustration of the fluorescence quenching mechanism of the FCDs in the absence (a) and (b) presence of Hg2+.
Comparison of different fluorescent probes for Hg2+ detection.

head.ce:title: Ultra-sensitive and selective Hg2+ detection based on fluorescent carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM) and Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials and Devices, Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM) and Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials and Devices, Soochow University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemisty Department, Northeast Normal University, Changchun 130024, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent carbon dots were efficiently synthesized by one-step sodium hydroxide-assisted reflux method from PEG and demonstrated to show high selectivity toward Hg2+ ions detection.

Fluorescent carbon dots (FCDs) were efficiently synthesized by one-step sodium hydroxide-assisted reflux method from poly(ethylene glycol) (PEG). The obtained FCDs exhibit excellent water-solubility and high stability. Under the UV irradiation, the FCDs could emit bright blue photoluminescence, and also they were found to show excellent up-conversion fluorescence. It was further demonstrated that such FCDs can serve as effective fluorescent sensing platform for Hg2+ ions detection with ultra-sensitivity and selectivity. The sensing system achieved a limit of detection as low as 1fM, which is much lower than all the previous reported sensing systems for Hg2+ ions detection. This FCDs sensing system has been successfully applied for the analysis of Hg2+ ions in water samples from river, lake, and tap water, showing good practical feasibility.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury (II) ion (Hg2+) is one of the most widespread heavy metal ions widely used in industrial and agriculture processes. Its high toxicity has severe pollution on the environment and brings great threat to the health of human beings [1a]. Therefore, from the viewpoint of environmental protection and health concerns, developing effective method for sensitive detection of trace amount of Hg2+ ions is very important and has attracted a significant amount of attention [1b]. In the past several years, many detection techniques including atomic absorption/emission spectrometry, Auger-electron spectroscopy, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry, polarography, electrochemical methods and fluorescence chemosensors have been established [1c–e]. Among them, fluoresence based methods display unique advantages of high sensitivity, great simplicity and fast response time [2a]. So far, numerous fluoresence based strategies have been reported for Hg2+ ions detection by employing organic small molecules [2b], DNA enzymes [2c], polymers [2d], single-walled carbon nanotubes [2e], metal complexes [2f] and semiconductor quantum dots [2g]. However, most of the traditional fluoresence based sensors are still limited with respect to the insufficient sensitivity. To the best of our knowledg, the lowest limit of detection (LOD) for Hg2+ ions detection was about 0.1nM, which was achieved by using an N-alkylaminopyrazole ligands as fluorescent probes [3]. In addition, the poor water-solubility of some fluorescent materials also limit their practical applications in the aqueous medium [4]. Thus, it is an important and urgent task to develop simple, cheap, ultra-sensitive and selective Hg2+ ions detection methods that can be used for the detection in water and the environment [5].

Recently, fluorescent carbon nanodots (FCDs) are being paid more and more attention due to their benign, abundance, low cost, good water-solubility, as well as abundant optical properties. Besides normal down converted photoluminescence (PL), FCDs were shown to possess excellent photostability and up-converted PL (UCPL), which enables their potential applications in catalysis and new energy fields [6–9]. Particularly, as a consequence of tunable PL and good biocompatibility, FCDs are becoming an attractive alternative for novel fluoresence marker [10a], fluorescent probe [10b], and optical sensor [10c]. Recently, Sun et al. demonstrated the use of FCDs as probes for sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ with a LOD as low as 0.23nM [11]. The sensing principle is based on fluorescence quenching of FCDs by Hg2+ ions presumably due to facilitating nonradiative electron/hole recombination annihilation through an effective electron or energy transfer process [11].

Herein, we reported a rapid and low cost method to synthesize FCDs from poly(ethylene glycol) (PEG 200) via one-step sodium hydroxide-assisted reflux treatment. We further demonstrate that such FCDs can serve as effective fluorescent sensing platform for Hg2+ ions detection with ultra-sensitivity and selectivity. The sensing system achieved a LOD as low as 1fM, which is much lower than all the previous reported sensing systems for Hg2+ ions detection.

All the chemicals were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich. In a typical experiment, 3.0g sodium hydroxide was added to a mixed solution of 50ml poly(ethylene glycol) (PEG 200) and 10ml distilled water to form a clear solution. Then the solution would become brown gradually in the progress of refluxing for 6h at 120°C. To remove the impurities, the raw solution was given a dialysis treatment using a semi-permeable membrane (MWCO 1000). At last, the obtained solution was changed to golden yellow color, implying we got the pure FCDs.

Based on the mechanism of fluorescence destroy, we used the prepared FCDs for metal ions detection. Firstly we confected various metal ions solution, such as AgNO3, FeCl3, Ni(NO3)2, Pb(NO3)2, CoCl2, Ni(NO3)2, MgSO4, HgCl2, Zn(NO3)2, CuSO4, BaCl2, MnSO4, Cd(NO3)2 and so on. In a typical detection experiment, 50μL FCDs solution was added into a certain amount of metal ions solution to form a 3mL clear solution. After mixing evenly, the PL spectra were detected at 350nm excitation wavelength.

TEM images of FCDs were carried out using a FEI/Philips Techal 12 BioTWIN TEM. The UV–Vis spectra were measured with an Agilent 8453 UV–Vis diode array spectraphotometer, while the PL study was conducted with a Horiba Jobin Yvon (FluoroMax 4) Luminescence Spectrometer and an optical fluorescent microscope (Leica DM4500B). We also obtained the Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectrum of FCDs with a Varian Spectrum GX spectrometer.

As illustrated in Fig. 1 (a), FCDs can be easily prepared by a sodium hydroxide-assisted refluxing method. Particularly, the present FCDs can freely disperse in water with transparent appearance without further ultrasonic dispersion. Fig. 1(a) shows the optical images of the FCDs under the illumination of sunlight and UV light (365nm, center), respectively. The bright blue PL of FCDs is strong enough to be easily seen with the naked eyes. Fig. 1(b) shows the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of the obtained FCDs, revealing that the FCDs are spherical with average size of 5nm, and the size histogram (inset of Fig. 1(b)) shows FCDs are well separated from each other. Fig. 1(c–f) display the typical fluorescent microscope images of FCDs. The blue, cyan, yellow, and red emissions are observed with the same FCDs sample under different excitations: 360nm, 390nm, 470nm, and 540nm, respectively.

Fig. 2 (a) shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the FCDs, from which we can see that the FCDs have the absorption peak in the range from 260 to 280nm. This peak represents the typical absorption of an aromatic pi system, which is similar to that of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons [12]. In order to find the origin of FCDs’ PL and water solubility, fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was employed to identify the functional groups of the prepared FCDs (Fig. 2(b)). The peaks around 3400, 1700, and 1100cm−1 are respectively accordance with the vibrations of COH, CO, and CO bonds, and the peaks at about 3000, 1500cm−1 are due to the CH stretch of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons [13]. According to the above FTIR spectrum results, we deduce that the surface of the obtained FCDs were occupied by hydrophilic groups (OH and COOH) from PEG. These hydrophilic groups lead to the good water dispersibility of FCDs, which greatly widen their further sensing application in aqueous system.

To explore the optical properties of the FCDs, the PL spectra of the sample (water as solvent) under UV and visible excitations from 300 to 600nm were detected. Fig. 2(c) shows the typical PL spectra of the FCDs, and the corresponding visible emissions of the FCDs covering from blue to red wavelength range can be observed. The maximum fluoresence emission intensity can be got under the excitation of 350nm. Interestingly, with increasing the excitation wavelengths after the maximum fluoresence emission, the fluorescent emission peaks gradually shift to longer wavelengths accompanied with decreased fluoresence emission intensity.

Besides the strong PL emission in the range of visible-to-near infrared (NIR), the FCDs also exhibit excellent UCPL property. Fig. 2(d) shows the PL spectra of FCDs excited by NIR wavelength light from 650 to 1000nm with the up-converted emissions located in the range from 400 to 650nm. Such a UCPL property of FCDs should be attributed to the multiphoton active process [9], and enables the further applications of FCDs in high performance photocatalyst design and biological luminescent labels in the NIR “water window”.

Since the FCDs were obtained directly from PEG, we envision that the formation mechanism of FCDs is as follows (supporting information, Fig. S1): (1) the decomposition of PEG; (2) the polymerization of carbon substances formed in the prior step; (3) the growth of carbon substances formed in step (3); (4) the oxidation process of grown carbon substances. It is known that ethanol or PEG can be decomposed into carbon substances or carbon nanotubes [14,15]. By definition, the reflux method in this synthesis is to thermally accelerate the reaction by conducting it at an elevated temperature, the given solvent will always boil at a high temperature. Under these conditions, the reactions often lead to products which cannot be obtained in a low-temperature chemical synthesis. In this work, no FCDs were observed in the absence of PEG, indicating that the FCDs were formed by the decomposition of PEG, which can be broken into carbon substances via polymerization, growth and oxidation processes. Similar phenomena were also reported for the synthesis of carbon nanotubes from PEG [15], preparation of FCDs by reflux of soot, microwave treatment of PEG [16,17] and thermal oxidation of other suitable molecular precursors [18–21].Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.materresbull.2013.03.015.

The synthetic process also indicated that a NaOH aqueous solution with high concentration was essential for the formation of the FCDs. No FCDs were detected by the TEM observations when the reactions were conducted in aqueous solutions with low NaOH concentration. We propose that the role of high concentration NaOH during the formation of FCDs is most likely as a chemical activation agent to activate PEG. Under the present reflux conditions, the activated PEG becomes easy to decompose into carbon substance and other products. The carbon substance with active surfaces is probably preferential for the polymerization and growth of FCDs under the reflux conditions. However, the true role of NaOH in the formation of FCDs is not clear. A detailed study is required to understand the mechanism of the FCDs. In comparison to the synthesis of FCDs reported before, our approach is a facile and green method, the FCDs were obtained in PEG solution, and no toxic reagents were used. The synthetic procedure involves none of the toxic organic solvents, initiators, or surfactants. This ensures that the as-prepared nanoparticles are nontoxic enabling their use in biochemistry or biodiagnostics, and so on.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ultra-sensitive and selective Hg2+ detection using FCDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Due to that the oxygen functional groups on the surface of FCDs can interact with metal ions to form complex compound by coordination, the FCDs provide a potential possibility to be used as fluorescent probe for metal ions detection. As the decreasing of oxygen functional groups coordinated with metal ions, the flourescent intensity will decline gradually [11]. For Hg2+ detection study, different concentrations of Hg2+ ions of 0–1μM were investigated, and Fig. 3 (a) shows the PL spectra corresponding to the FCDs systems at different Hg2+ ions concentrations. It can be clearly seen that the FCDs exhibits a strong emission at 450nm, and the fluorescence intensity of the mixture show obvious decrease with increasing the Hg2+ ions concentration. This observation can be attributed to that Hg2+ ions can quench the fluorescence of FCDs presumably via electron or energy transfer [22]. Fig. 3(b) shows the dependence of F/F
0 (F, F
0 are the PL intensities of FCDs at 450nm in the absence and presence of Hg2+ ions) on the concentrations of Hg2+ ions within the range of 0–5fM (excitation wavelength: 350nm), and we can see that the FCDs’ lowest LOD for Hg2+ ions can be achieved to 1fM. It's worth noting that the PL intensity of FCDs decreased acutely dependent on the Hg2+ ions concentration lower than 10nM. Inset in Fig. 3(b) depicts the lineal detecting plot within the range of 0–10nM, which means the prepared FCDs have excellent sensitivity to Hg2+ ions and can used to realize the ultrasensitive detection of trace amount of Hg2+ ions. Table 1 shows the peformance comparison between different Hg2+ ions sensing systems reported and FCDs.

For selectivity study of the prepared FCDs towards different metal ions, we recorded the PL spectra of FCDs solution in the absence and presence of various metal ions, including Pb2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Hg2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Ba2+ Mn2+ and Cd2+, respectively (metal ions concentrations all kept at 1 μM). As Fig. 4 (a) shown, these metal ions had different influence on the PL intensity of the FCDs, and the lowest PL intensity was notably achieved for the FCDs upon the addition of Hg2+ ions. Fig. 4(b) clearly displays the strong contrast for the fine detection selectivity of FCDs for Hg2+ ions. The outstanding selectivity and specificity can be probably attributed to that Hg2+ ions has a stronger affinity toward the carboxylic group [11] and a stronger quenching effect on the PL of FCDs than other metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Efficient Hg2+ detection using FCDs in water samples from surroundings
ce:section.ce:para:
The sensing capability of FCDs for detecting Hg2+ ions was evaluated in natural water samples, which were collected from the river surrounded Soochow University, tap water, and Dushu Lake in Suzhou, respectively. The water samples were used as collected without any pretreatment. Fig. 5 shows the detecting results for the river surrounded Soochow University. We can see from Fig. 5(a) that the PL intensity decreases with increasing the concentration of Hg2+ from 5 to 50fM. Fig. 5(b) shows the calibration curve by plotting the values of F/F
0 versus the concentrations of Hg2+ ions within the range of 0–50fM. It clearly shows that although some organic/inorganic impurities and minerals existed in the river sample, the FCDs sensing system can still distinguish between fresh river water and that spiked with 5fM Hg2+ ions, satisfying the practical Hg2+ ions detection in real samples. Fig. S2 depicts the detecting results of FCDs for tap water and Dushu Lake in Suzhou, respectively, clearly showing that the LOD of FCDs in these two water samples also reached to 5fM. Such a low LOD value imply that the FCDs can be used as a new kind of Hg2+ ions sensing probe, with practically feasibility for Hg2+ detection in real water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching mechanism of FCDs-Hg2+ system
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 depicts the proposed fluorescence quenching mechanism for this FCDs-Hg2+ system, and it is similar to that reported by Zhou and Lin [22]. Initially, the free FCDs showed strong fluorescence in aqueous solution. However, the fluorescence of FCDs was quenched significantly in the presence of Hg2+ through the charge transfer process. The luminescence emission of FCDs arises from the radiative recombination of excitons, which is similar to the other QDs [22]. Then it was deduced that Hg2+ can quench the fluorescence of FCDs due to facilitating non-radiative electron/hole recombination annihilation through an effective electron transfer process [22,23]. In order to further understand the fluorescence quenching mechanism, we conducted the PL and photoinduced electron transfer properties of FCDs. Fig. S3 shows the luminescence decays (300nm excitation, monitored with 550nm narrow bandpass filter) of the FCDs in the absence and presence of Hg2+ ions. It can be obviously observed that the fluorescence lifetime of the FCDs (the black line) is 6.0ns after fitting, reflecting a fast exciton recombination process. After the additions of Hg2+ ions (the red line), the lifetime of FCDs decreases to 1.0ns. The significantly reduced lifetime indicates a dynamics quenching occurs, and further confirms that there is an ultrafast electron transfer process in the FCDs-Hg2+ system. This fluorescence quenching mechanism can also explain the phenomenon of Hg2+ detection by FCDs.

In summary, a novel, ulta-selective and sensitive fluorescent probe for label-free Hg2+ ions detection was developed based on FCDs, which were prepared by a one-step sodium hydroxide-assisted reflux method from PEG. The LOD of FCDs for Hg2+ ions detection was achieved to 1fM, which is the lowest value so far. This FCDs-based sensing system shows high selectivity toward Hg2+ ions detection and has been successfully applied for the analysis of water samples from river, lake, and tap water. As known, FCDs functionalized by different groups can be obtained from different precusors. We therefore expect that our finding will stimulate further development of FCDs based sensors on the basis of the coordination between the functional goups and metal ions. Combining the good water-solubility, excellent PL, favourable biocompatibility and so on, the obtained FCDs may serve as a promising candidate for new type fluoresence probes, bio-sensors, biomedical imaging, and drug delivery.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Basic Research Program of China (973 Program) (2012CB825800, 2013CB932702), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51132006, 21073127, 21071104), the Specialized Research Fund for the Doctoral Program of Higher Education (20123201110018), a Suzhou Planning Project of Science and Technology (ZXG2012028), a Foundation for the Author of National Excellent Doctoral Dissertation of China (200929), and a project funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions.
